text:
Grand sustainability challenges tend to be cross-sectorial and often span far beyond the scope of a single policy field. When innovative technologies become available to tackle such challenges they may create the conditions for the formation of a new policy subsystem at the crossroads of several existing policy fields. This paper develops a framework for managing multi-system settings.

Beliefs and coalitions in the emerging policy domain of hydrogen technology in Germany This paper empirically studies the process of belief formation and coalition building within the emerging policy domain of hydrogen technology in Germany. Hydrogen technology is an intersection issue for the energy, transport, buildings and industry sectors, and therefore a large number of different types of actors are involved in this issue. Based on an in-depth analysis of the coalition building process in this policy domain our empirical results show the existence of two advocacy coalitions. The first one is described as an “unusual” coalition consisting of companies, non-governmental organisations (NGOs) and research institutes. The second one is described as an “expected” coalition of representatives of the gas and heating sector.

The article reveals the complex coalition building and contentious politics underlying efforts to shape the evolving hydrogen economy. While the various interests disagreed on a range of topics, from fuel production and application to import criteria, there was a significant degree of convergence concerning the importance of hydrogen technology and the need for state involvement. Based on an interpretive analysis blending the Advocacy Coalition Framework (ACF) and the politics of transitions, the study demonstrates how in a new policy subsystem coalition-building is shaped by a range of factors, including the self-interest of stakeholders (defined by their primary or secondary affiliation to the sector concerned), the technology in question, trust-building and previous cooperative experiences fostered by institutional links. The analysis sheds light on the formative stages of policy-making processes in complex integrated multi-systems.
